posts.title: Multi-agent systems are the new microservices
posts.hook: Watched three teams this quarter rip out their single-agent setups and replace them with multi-agent orchestration.
posts.body:
Watched three teams this quarter rip out their single-agent setups and replace them with multi-agent orchestration.

The pattern is identical to what happened with monoliths --> microservices a decade ago.

One big agent that does everything sounds clean in a demo. In production, it hallucinates on edge cases, burns tokens on simple tasks, and can't be debugged because the context window is a black box.

Split it into focused agents, each with a narrow job and its own prompt, and suddenly you can test them individually. Swap one out without rewriting the whole system. Run the cheap model on the easy tasks and the expensive model on the hard ones.

The teams getting this right aren't building "AI platforms." They're building pipelines where agents are just nodes. Data flows in, decisions flow out, humans review the ones that matter.

The teams getting it wrong are still trying to make one mega-prompt do everything. Same mistake we made with monoliths. Same painful migration coming.

If you're building with agents right now, start small. One agent, one job. Chain them later. The orchestration layer is the product, not the individual agent.
posts.cta: Anyone else seeing this pattern? Curious what orchestration frameworks people are actually using in production.

inspired_by.author: Swyx
inspired_by.summary: Swyx discusses the evolution of AI agents, highlighting how multi-agent systems are outperforming single agents in complex tasks. He shares insights from recent benchmarks showing 2x efficiency gains in automation workflows. Emphasizes the need for better developer tools to orchestrate these agents at scale.

remixes.label: lessons-from-failure
remixes.hook: Our single agent worked perfectly for 6 months. Then it hit production traffic and fell apart in ways we didn't predict.
remixes.body:
Our single agent worked perfectly for 6 months. Then it hit production traffic and fell apart in ways we didn't predict.

The failure mode wasn't hallucination. It was resource contention. One complex query would eat 80% of the context window, and every subsequent task in the pipeline got dumber answers because it was working with scraps.

So we split it. One agent for intake and classification. One for research and enrichment. One for draft generation. One for quality checks.

Each agent now has a focused context, a clear contract for inputs and outputs, and its own error boundary. Sound familiar? It should. This is the exact decomposition pattern backend engineers learned with services a decade ago.

The tradeoffs are the same too. More moving parts. More network calls. More things to monitor. But each piece is testable in isolation, and when something breaks at 2am, you know exactly which agent failed and why.

The teams doing this well aren't AI researchers. They're engineers who already survived the monolith-to-services migration and recognize the same symptoms. Slow debugging, token bloat, context pollution.

If your single agent works today, great. Keep shipping. But bookmark this for when it doesn't.
remixes.cta: What was the breaking point that made you split your agent into multiple specialized ones?

remixes.label: tactical-breakdown
remixes.hook: Here's the actual architecture behind a multi-agent system that handles 50k tasks a day without falling over.
remixes.body:
Here's the actual architecture behind a multi-agent system that handles 50k tasks a day without falling over.

Router agent sits at the front. It classifies incoming requests and sends them to the right specialist. No generation, no reasoning. Just classification. Fast, cheap, runs on a small model.

Specialist agents handle domains. One for code review. One for customer support triage. One for data extraction. Each has a narrow system prompt and a curated tool set. They can't wander into territory they shouldn't touch.

Validator agent sits at the end. It checks outputs against a schema, flags confidence scores below threshold, and routes failures back for retry with additional context.

Orchestrator ties it together. Manages state, handles timeouts, logs every handoff for debugging. This is the piece most teams skip, and it's why their multi-agent setup becomes harder to debug than the monolith it replaced.

The key insight: each agent is stateless. State lives in the orchestrator. This means you can swap, scale, or kill any individual agent without cascading failures.

Total token cost is actually lower than the single-agent version because small models handle most of the routing and validation work.
remixes.cta: Drop your multi-agent architecture diagram. I want to see how others are structuring the orchestration layer.

tones.advisor:
The shift from single-agent to multi-agent architectures is accelerating, and the teams making this move now are building significant operational advantages.

Here's what I recommend paying attention to. A monolithic agent looks elegant in demos. In production, it creates three predictable failure modes: context window saturation on complex queries, token waste on simple tasks that don't need a large model, and debugging nightmares because every failure is buried in one massive conversation thread.

The multi-agent pattern solves each of these. Decompose by function. A classifier agent routes requests. Specialist agents handle domains. A validator agent checks outputs. An orchestrator manages state.

The parallel to microservices isn't just an analogy. It's a direct mapping. You get the same benefits: independent scaling, isolated failures, testable units. And you inherit the same costs: network overhead, distributed tracing, contract management between agents.

My guidance for teams considering this transition: don't rearchitect everything at once. Start by extracting your most failure-prone capability into a standalone agent with clear input/output contracts. Prove the pattern works in your environment. Then decompose further as pain points emerge.

The organizations that will lead in AI operations over the next 18 months are the ones treating agent architecture with the same rigor they applied to service-oriented architecture.
tones.provocateur:
Everyone's excited about multi-agent systems like it's some breakthrough. It's not. It's the same lesson software engineering already taught us, and most teams are going to learn it the hard way. Again.

Your single agent is a monolith. It does everything, knows everything, and when it breaks, nobody can figure out why. We solved this problem in 2014 with microservices. Now we're solving it again because AI teams think they're special.

Here's the uncomfortable part. Half the teams adopting multi-agent orchestration right now are doing it because it sounds impressive on a blog post, not because they've hit actual scaling problems. They're pre-optimizing for complexity they don't have yet.

And the frameworks? Most of them are just fancy wrappers around prompt chaining with a coordinator loop. You could build the same thing with a Python script and a dictionary. But that doesn't get conference talks.

The teams doing this right aren't talking about it. They're quietly decomposing their agents based on actual failure modes in production. Not theoretical architectural diagrams.

If your single agent works, keep it. If it's breaking in specific, repeatable ways, extract those failure points into specialized agents. That's engineering. Everything else is architecture theater.

posts.title: GTM engineering is not a rebrand of sales ops
posts.hook: Sales ops manages Salesforce fields. GTM engineering builds the machine that fills them.
posts.body:
Sales ops manages Salesforce fields. GTM engineering builds the machine that fills them.

I keep seeing job posts for "GTM Engineer" that read like sales ops with a new title. Configure CRM. Build reports. Maintain sequences.

That's not engineering. That's administration.

A GTM engineering team should be building:

✅ Automated enrichment pipelines that score and route accounts before a human touches them
✅ Signal detection that catches buying intent from job postings, tech stack changes, funding rounds
✅ Waterfall data strategies across 4+ providers so your reps never say "I don't have the email"
✅ Feedback loops from closed-won back to ICP scoring so the model gets smarter every quarter

The difference is building systems vs. maintaining tools. One compounds. The other doesn't.

Best GTM engineering hires I've seen came from two backgrounds. Former SDRs who taught themselves to code because they got tired of doing things manually. And engineers who spent time in a revenue role and understood the pain.

The common thread is they've done the manual work. They know what to automate because they've felt the repetition in their hands.
posts.cta: If you're building a GTM engineering function, what was the first thing you automated?

inspired_by.author: Sami Rahman
inspired_by.summary: Sami shares a detailed framework for building GTM engineering teams, emphasizing the integration of data, automation, and cross-functional alignment to accelerate revenue growth. He outlines key roles, tools like HubSpot and Gong, and metrics for success in high-growth startups. The post includes actionable steps for founders to implement GTM engineering without over-hiring.

remixes.label: hiring-lens
remixes.hook: I've reviewed 40+ "GTM Engineer" job descriptions this year. Only 6 of them actually described engineering work.
remixes.body:
I've reviewed 40+ "GTM Engineer" job descriptions this year. Only 6 of them actually described engineering work.

The rest? Salesforce admin with a title bump. "Manage CRM hygiene." "Build dashboards in Looker." "Maintain outbound sequences." That's operations. Valuable work, but it's not engineering.

The 6 real GTM engineering roles had something different in common. They all listed building, not configuring.

Build automated enrichment pipelines that process accounts before any human touches them. Build scoring models that route leads based on signals, not gut feel. Build attribution systems that connect marketing spend to closed revenue with actual data, not vibes.

The distinction matters because it changes who you hire. A GTM engineer needs to write code, design data pipelines, and think about systems architecture. They need to understand APIs, webhooks, data schemas, and orchestration.

A sales ops person needs to understand Salesforce flows, reporting tools, and process design. Also critical work. But different skills, different output, different career path.

When companies blur these roles, they end up with engineers doing admin work or admins drowning in engineering problems. Both lose.

If your GTM engineer job post doesn't mention a single programming language or data pipeline, you're hiring sales ops. Which is fine. Just call it what it is.
remixes.cta: Show me your GTM engineer job description. I'll tell you if it's actually engineering or admin with a new title.

remixes.label: build-the-machine
remixes.hook: Your revenue team has 12 tools and zero pipelines connecting them. That's not a stack. That's a pile.
remixes.body:
Your revenue team has 12 tools and zero pipelines connecting them. That's not a stack. That's a pile.

I see this constantly. A company buys Outreach, Apollo, Clay, Salesforce, Gong, 6sense, Clearbit, and a handful of others. Each tool works fine on its own. But data flows between them through manual exports, copy-paste, and "can you update this spreadsheet?"

That's where GTM engineering comes in. Not configuring each tool. Building the connective tissue between them.

An enrichment pipeline that takes a new account, hits 4 data providers, scores the result, and routes it to the right sequence without a human touching it. A feedback loop that takes closed-won data and retrains your ICP scoring model. An alert system that pings your AE when a target account hits your pricing page three times in a week.

This is systems work. It requires thinking about data models, error handling, rate limits, and failure modes. It requires writing actual code, not just clicking through a UI.

The companies winning at GTM right now aren't the ones with the most tools. They're the ones who connected their tools into a machine that runs while the team sleeps.

Sales ops keeps the tools running. GTM engineering makes them talk to each other.
remixes.cta: What's the first pipeline you'd build if you had a GTM engineer starting Monday?

tones.advisor:
There's a critical distinction forming in revenue organizations right now, and getting it wrong will cost you both talent and pipeline.

Sales operations and GTM engineering are different functions. They share a revenue mandate but diverge in execution. Sales ops manages systems of record. They configure CRM fields, build reports, maintain sequences, and ensure process compliance. This is essential work that keeps the revenue machine running day to day.

GTM engineering builds the automated infrastructure that feeds those systems. Enrichment pipelines that score and route accounts before a human intervenes. Signal detection systems that identify buying intent across data sources. Attribution models that connect spend to revenue with real data.

My recommendation: separate these functions clearly in your org design. When you conflate them, you get one of two outcomes. Either an engineer spends their time on CRM hygiene instead of building pipeline infrastructure, or an ops person gets handed engineering problems they aren't equipped to solve.

The hiring criteria are different too. GTM engineers need programming skills, data pipeline experience, and API fluency. Sales ops needs process design, tool administration, and cross-functional communication.

Both roles are valuable. But if your GTM engineer job post reads like a Salesforce admin role with a trendy title, you'll attract the wrong candidates and wonder why your revenue infrastructure never improves.
tones.provocateur:
Let's be honest. Most companies slapping "GTM Engineer" on a job title are just trying to make sales ops sound sexier for recruiting purposes.

The job post says GTM Engineer. The actual work is maintaining Salesforce fields, pulling reports for leadership, and fixing broken Outreach sequences when someone changes the email template. That's admin work. Calling it engineering doesn't make it engineering.

And the candidates know it. Actual engineers read these posts, see "maintain CRM hygiene" in the requirements, and close the tab. So you end up hiring ops people into engineering titles, and then you're confused when nobody builds anything.

Here's the test. Does this role write code that runs in production? Does it build data pipelines that process accounts autonomously? Does it architect systems that generate pipeline without human intervention?

If yes, it's engineering. If the work is configuring tools that other people built, it's operations.

Stop diluting the title. It hurts the people doing real GTM engineering work, and it misleads ops professionals who take the role expecting one thing and get handed architecture problems they never signed up for.

You need both functions. Just be honest about which one you're hiring for.

posts.title: Shipping beats architecture every time
posts.hook: I spent 3 weeks designing the perfect architecture for a tool. Then shipped a janky v1 in a weekend that got more users than anything I'd planned.
posts.body:
I spent 3 weeks designing the perfect architecture for a tool. Then shipped a janky v1 in a weekend that got more users than anything I'd planned.

This keeps happening.

The planned version had a database schema with 14 tables, a queue system, proper auth, role-based access. Beautiful on a whiteboard.

The weekend version was a single Python script, a JSON file for storage, and a Vercel deployment. Ugly. Functional. People used it.

I think there are two modes of building and most people default to the wrong one.

Mode 1: Design the system, then find the users.
Mode 2: Find the pain, ship the fix, clean it up when it matters.

The solo builders who ship 10+ products aren't better engineers. They just skip the architecture phase until they have proof someone cares.

I've been running my own stack for a few weeks now. Blog generator, content pipeline, CRM automations, SEO tracking. None of it started with a plan. All of it started with "I'm tired of doing this manually."

The production version looks nothing like v1. But v1 is the only reason the production version exists.
posts.cta: What's something you over-architected that should've been a weekend project?

inspired_by.author: Pieter Levels
inspired_by.summary: Pieter shares his journey as a solo founder building and shipping multiple products like Nomad List and Remote OK without VC funding. He emphasizes transparency in revenue figures and development updates to inspire other indie hackers. The post details his latest product launch and lessons learned from rapid iteration.

remixes.label: anti-planning-manifesto
remixes.hook: My best-performing projects all have one thing in common: I was embarrassed to show them to anyone.
remixes.body:
My best-performing projects all have one thing in common: I was embarrassed to show them to anyone.

The JSON file that served as a database. The hardcoded config values. The CSS that only worked at one screen size. The deploy script that was literally just a bash alias.

Every single one got more traction than the "properly architected" versions that came after.

Because the ugly version shipped on Tuesday. The proper version shipped never. Or shipped three months later to an audience that had already moved on.

There's a specific kind of procrastination that disguises itself as professionalism. "We need proper auth first." "Let me set up the CI pipeline." "I should design the database schema before writing any code."

All true. All valid. All excuses to avoid the scary part, which is putting something in front of real people who might not like it.

The weekend project works because constraints force decisions. No time to debate database choices. Pick SQLite. No time for auth. Ship without it. No time for tests. Watch the logs manually.

You learn more from 48 hours of real user feedback than 3 weeks of architecture diagrams.

This doesn't mean architecture doesn't matter. It means architecture should follow traction, not precede it. Build the right thing first. Build it right second.
remixes.cta: What's the most embarrassing v1 you shipped that actually worked?

remixes.label: contrarian-flip
remixes.hook: Architecture doesn't kill projects. But it kills momentum. And momentum is the only thing that keeps solo builders alive.
remixes.body:
Architecture doesn't kill projects. But it kills momentum. And momentum is the only thing that keeps solo builders alive.

I've tracked my own project history for the last two years. 14 projects started with a planning phase. 3 shipped. 23 projects started with a weekend hack. 19 shipped.

The numbers are comically lopsided.

It's not that planning is bad. It's that planning has a hidden cost that nobody talks about. Every hour spent designing is an hour where you haven't validated whether anyone cares. And the longer you plan, the more invested you become in the plan itself, making you resistant to the feedback that would have reshaped it.

The weekend hack has no ego. You built it in two days. If users hate it, you throw it away and try something different. If they love one feature and ignore the rest, you double down on what works.

Try this. Next time you have an idea, give yourself 48 hours to ship a working version. Not a mockup. Not a landing page. A real thing that does one thing.

Use the simplest tools you know. Don't learn a new framework. Don't set up infrastructure. JSON files, SQLite, a single deployment command.

If it gets traction, then plan. If it doesn't, you lost a weekend instead of a month.

Shipping is a muscle. Architecture planning can become a comfortable alternative to exercising it.
remixes.cta: Give yourself a 48-hour shipping challenge this weekend. What would you build?

tones.advisor:
One of the most common patterns I see with technical founders and solo builders is over-investment in architecture before validation.

Here's what typically happens. You have a product idea. You start with the database schema because that feels like the responsible thing to do. Then auth, because security matters. Then a CI pipeline, because you want clean deploys. Three weeks later, you have beautiful infrastructure and zero users.

Meanwhile, the competitor who shipped a Python script with a JSON file for storage last weekend already has 200 users giving them feedback.

My recommendation: flip the sequence. Ship first, architect second.

Build the simplest version that proves the core value proposition. One file, one deployment, no auth, no database optimization. Put it in front of real people. Collect feedback for two weeks.

Then, and only then, invest in architecture. At that point you know which features matter, what scale you actually need, and where the real technical challenges are. Your architecture decisions will be informed by reality instead of assumptions.

This applies to features within existing products too. The fastest way to validate whether users want something is to build a rough version and watch what happens. Polish the things that get traction. Kill the things that don't.

The cost of shipping something ugly is embarrassment. The cost of not shipping is irrelevance.
tones.provocateur:
Your architecture diagram is a security blanket. Let's just say it.

You're not spending three weeks on database schema design because the project requires it. You're doing it because shipping is scary and planning feels productive. Planning lets you stay in the comfortable world where everything works perfectly in your head.

I spent 3 weeks designing a 14-table schema, a queue system, role-based access, the whole thing. Beautiful on a whiteboard. Then I shipped a janky version in a weekend. Single Python script. JSON for storage. Vercel deployment.

Guess which one got users. Not the one with the architecture diagram.

Here's . Most projects die not because the architecture was wrong, but because they never shipped. The graveyard of side projects is full of perfectly designed systems that nobody ever used.

And the "proper engineering" crowd will hate this. "But what about technical debt?" Technical debt requires having users first. An unused codebase doesn't have debt. It has nothing.

Ship the embarrassing version. Let real people break it. Then fix the things that actually matter instead of the things you imagined would matter.

Architecture follows traction. Not the other way around.

posts.title: Outbound automation is a race to the spam folder
posts.hook: The more automated your outbound gets, the less your prospects trust it.
posts.body:
The more automated your outbound gets, the less your prospects trust it.

I automated outbound for years. Built Clay tables that enriched leads across 6 providers, wrote sequences with AI personalization at scale, warmed domains properly, rotated inboxes.

And reply rates still dropped. Every quarter.

Not because the tech was bad. Because everyone else was using the same tech. When every SDR has AI-written emails that reference your latest blog post and your company's Series B, the "personalization" becomes noise.

What actually moved pipeline last quarter was different. Fewer emails, more targeted. Manual research on the 50 accounts that actually showed buying signals, not the 5,000 that matched a firmographic filter.

The automation didn't go away. It just moved upstream. Automate the research, the enrichment, the signal detection. Keep the actual outreach human and specific enough that it can't be replicated by a prompt.

The irony of outbound automation is that the best use of it is to figure out which 2% of your list deserves a handwritten email.

Tools are force multipliers. They multiply whatever you point them at. Point them at volume and you get scaled spam. Point them at precision and you get pipeline.
posts.cta: What's your ratio of automated vs. manual outreach right now?

inspired_by.author: Josh Braun
inspired_by.summary: Josh critiques overhyped outbound automation tools and advocates for a human-centered approach combined with smart tech stacks. He provides examples of sequences that convert at 20%+ rates by personalizing at scale using AI without sounding robotic. The post warns against common pitfalls in sales ops automation.

remixes.label: death-of-cold-outbound
remixes.hook: Cold outbound isn't broken because of bad copy. It's broken because recipients can smell automation from the subject line.
remixes.body:
Cold outbound isn't broken because of bad copy. It's broken because recipients can smell automation from the subject line.

I ran an experiment last month. Sent 500 emails from our automated Clay pipeline. Personalized with 6 data providers, custom first lines, company-specific angles. State of the art stuff.

Then I sent 50 emails manually. Read the prospect's recent LinkedIn posts, referenced something specific, wrote like a human who actually cared about their problem.

The manual emails had 4x the reply rate. Not because they were better written. Because they were real.

Here's what happened to outbound over the last three years. The tools got better, so everyone adopted them. Now every inbox gets 30 "personalized" emails a day that all sound the same. "I noticed your company just raised a Series B" is not personal anymore. It's a template with a variable.

The automation arms race created an environment where authentic human communication is now the competitive advantage. Which is ironic, because that's where we started before all the tools existed.

I'm not saying shut down your sequences. I'm saying the ratio needs to flip. Instead of 90% automated, 10% manual, try 30% automated for bottom-funnel nurture and 70% high-touch for accounts that actually matter.

The best outbound teams I know now spend more time researching 10 accounts than blasting 1,000.
remixes.cta: How many of your replies this quarter came from automated vs. manual outreach? Be honest.

remixes.label: signal-over-volume
remixes.hook: We cut our outbound volume by 80% and pipeline went up. Here's why that math works.
remixes.body:
We cut our outbound volume by 80% and pipeline went up. Here's why that math works.

Last year we sent 12,000 outbound emails a month. Warmed domains, rotated inboxes, personalized with Clay, the whole playbook. Generated about 40 meetings a month. That's a 0.33% conversion rate.

This year we send 2,400 emails a month. But we only email accounts showing active buying signals. Website visits, job postings that match our use case, tech stack changes, funding events with a specific thesis match.

We generate 55 meetings a month now. 2.3% conversion rate.

The math is simple. When you email everyone who fits your ICP, most of them aren't in a buying cycle. Your email is noise. When you email people who are actively looking for a solution, your email is relevant.

The hard part isn't sending fewer emails. It's building the signal detection infrastructure that tells you which accounts are in-market right now. That requires intent data, website tracking, job board monitoring, and a scoring model that actually works.

This is where GTM engineering pays off. The signal layer isn't something you buy off the shelf. You build it by connecting data sources, writing scoring logic, and refining based on what actually converts.

Volume was the strategy when we had no data. Now we have data. Use it.
remixes.cta: What buying signals are you tracking to prioritize outbound? I'll share our full signal stack if there's interest.

tones.builder:
The more automated your outbound gets, the less your prospects trust it.

I automated outbound for years. Built Clay tables that enriched leads across 6 providers, wrote sequences with AI personalization at scale, warmed domains properly, rotated inboxes.

And reply rates still dropped. Every quarter.

Not because the tech was bad. Because everyone else was using the same tech. When every SDR has AI-written emails that reference your latest blog post and your company's Series B, the "personalization" becomes noise.

What actually moved pipeline last quarter was different. Fewer emails, more targeted. Manual research on the 50 accounts that actually showed buying signals, not the 5,000 that matched a firmographic filter.

The automation didn't go away. It just moved upstream. Automate the research, the enrichment, the signal detection. Keep the actual outreach human and specific enough that it can't be replicated by a prompt.

The irony of outbound automation is that the best use of it is to figure out which 2% of your list deserves a handwritten email.

Tools are force multipliers. They multiply whatever you point them at. Point them at volume and you get scaled spam. Point them at precision and you get pipeline.
tones.advisor:
Outbound automation has reached a point of diminishing returns, and the data supports a strategic pivot for most B2B teams.

Here's the pattern I see consistently. Teams invest heavily in automated outbound infrastructure. Clay for enrichment, AI for personalization, domain warming, inbox rotation. The technical execution is excellent. And reply rates still decline quarter over quarter.

The reason is market saturation. When every company uses the same enrichment providers and personalization techniques, the output converges. Recipients now receive dozens of emails that all sound algorithmically generated, because they are.

My recommendation is to shift from volume-based to signal-based outbound. Instead of emailing every account that fits your ICP, invest in building infrastructure that identifies which accounts are actively in a buying cycle. Intent signals, website behavior, job postings, tech stack changes, funding events.

Then apply your human effort to those high-signal accounts. Research them properly. Write messages that reference specific, recent context. Make each touchpoint feel like it came from someone who actually understands their situation.

The ratio I recommend for most B2B teams: 30% automated sequences for bottom-funnel nurture and known-intent accounts. 70% high-touch, research-driven outreach for your top target accounts.

This approach requires more investment per account but generates significantly higher conversion rates and better customer relationships from the first touch.

Your outbound automation stack is impressive. It's also the reason your emails go straight to trash.

Every SDR team on LinkedIn is bragging about their Clay enrichment pipeline. Custom first lines from 6 data providers. AI-written body copy. Warmed domains. Rotating inboxes. The whole production.

And they're all producing the exact same output. Which means your prospect gets 30 emails a week that all start with "I noticed your company just..." and they delete every single one without reading past the first line.

You built a machine that generates spam at scale and called it a growth engine.

The uncomfortable reality: the best-performing outbound teams I know send fewer emails than your SDR sends before lunch. They spend an hour researching one account instead of 30 seconds personalizing 100.

And their reply rates are 5 to 10x higher. Because actual human attention is now the scarcest resource in every inbox.

The automation tools didn't fail. They succeeded so completely that they destroyed the channel. When everyone has the same weapon, it stops being an advantage.

Want better outbound results? Send less. Research more. Write emails that couldn't have been generated by the same tool your competitor is using.

Or keep optimizing your Clay table while reply rates drop another 20% next quarter.

posts.title: Your AI agent's biggest bottleneck isn't the model
posts.hook: Swapped from GPT-4 to Claude to Gemini on the same agent pipeline. Performance barely changed.
posts.body:
Swapped from GPT-4 to Claude to Gemini on the same agent pipeline. Performance barely changed.

Know what made the biggest difference? The data going in.

Teams are spending weeks evaluating which model to use for their agent. Running benchmarks, comparing pricing, A/B testing response quality. Meanwhile their input data is messy, their prompts are vague, and their error handling is nonexistent.

The model is maybe 20% of the outcome. The other 80% is infrastructure nobody wants to build.

Context assembly. How do you get the right information into the prompt without blowing the token budget? Most agents fail because they're either starved of context or drowning in it.

Error recovery. What happens when the agent hallucinates? When the API times out? When the output doesn't match the expected schema? In production, this happens constantly.

Observability. Can you see what the agent decided and why? If you can't replay a failed run and understand the reasoning, you can't fix it.

I run multiple agents in production. The ones that work well aren't the ones with the best model. They're the ones with the most boring, unglamorous infrastructure around them. Structured inputs, typed outputs, retry logic, logging on every step.

The model is the easy part. Everything around it is the product.
posts.cta: What's the most underrated piece of your agent infrastructure?

He critiques current cloud providers and advocates for specialized AI infra stacks that reduce latency by 40%. Provides a roadmap for developers building agentic systems.

remixes.label: input-data-thesis
remixes.hook: Your agent is only as good as the worst data source feeding it. Most teams never audit that layer.
remixes.body:
Your agent is only as good as the worst data source feeding it. Most teams never audit that layer.

I reviewed 8 agent pipelines last quarter across different companies. Every team had spent significant time on model selection. Benchmarks, pricing comparisons, quality evaluations. Real diligence.

But when I looked at what was actually flowing into the agent, it was a mess. Scraped web data with broken HTML tags still embedded. CRM records with fields that hadn't been updated in months. PDFs that were OCR'd with 15% error rates. API responses being passed in raw with no schema validation.

The agent was doing its best with garbage inputs and everyone was blaming the model.

Here's the diagnostic I now run on every agent project. Take your worst-performing queries. The ones where the agent gives bad answers or hallucinates. Don't look at the prompt first. Look at the data. Print out every piece of context the agent received for that query.

80% of the time, the answer is right there. Missing data, stale data, malformed data, or irrelevant data that's diluting the useful context.

The unsexy fix: build a data quality layer between your sources and your agent. Validate schemas. Check freshness dates. Strip formatting artifacts. Score relevance before stuffing everything into the context window.

Model upgrades give you incremental improvement. Data quality gives you step-function improvement.
remixes.cta: When was the last time you audited what data is actually flowing into your agent's context window?

remixes.label: wrong-optimization
remixes.hook: Teams are spending $50K on model evaluations and $0 on the data pipeline feeding the model. The ROI is upside down.
remixes.body:
Teams are spending $50K on model evaluations and $0 on the data pipeline feeding the model. The ROI is upside down.

I watched a team spend six weeks running an evaluation suite across four models. Custom benchmarks, human evaluators, latency testing, cost projections. Thorough, professional work.

They picked the winning model. Deployed it. Performance was mediocre.

Then someone on the team spent a Friday afternoon cleaning up the data pipeline. Removed duplicate documents from the retrieval index. Added freshness filters so the agent stopped citing 2-year-old docs. Fixed a parsing bug that was corrupting table data.

Agent performance improved more from that Friday afternoon than from the entire six-week model evaluation.

This isn't an isolated story. It's the pattern everywhere. The model is the thing we can see, measure, and compare. It feels like the lever. The data pipeline is invisible, boring, and nobody writes blog posts about cleaning up their ETL.

But here's the math. Switching from one frontier model to another gives you maybe a 5-15% improvement on most tasks. Fixing your data inputs, making sure the agent receives clean, relevant, fresh context, can improve output quality by 50% or more.

The order of operations should be: get your data right, get your prompts right, get your error handling right, and then, maybe, evaluate models.

Most teams have it completely backwards.
remixes.cta: What's the most impactful non-model change you've made to an agent system?

tones.advisor:
After working with multiple agent implementations, I can tell you confidently that model selection is rarely the highest-leverage optimization available to teams.

Here's what I consistently see. Organizations invest weeks in model evaluation. Benchmarks, A/B tests, cost analysis, latency profiling. All worthwhile activities. But they account for maybe 10-15% of the variance in agent output quality.

The remaining 85% comes from three areas that get far less attention.

First, data quality. What context is actually flowing into the agent's prompt? Is it clean, relevant, and current? Most teams have never audited this layer. Stale data, malformed inputs, and irrelevant context are common and directly cause the failures teams attribute to model limitations.

Second, prompt engineering at the system level. Not clever tricks, but clear instructions, well-structured context, and explicit output formatting. This is where domain expertise translates into agent capability.

Third, error handling and fallback logic. What happens when the agent produces low-confidence output? Most implementations just return whatever the model generates. Adding confidence scoring, retry logic with refined context, and graceful degradation paths dramatically improves reliability.

My recommended priority order: audit and fix your data pipeline, refine your system prompts with real failure cases, build robust error handling, and then evaluate models. You'll likely find that your current model performs significantly better once the surrounding infrastructure is solid.

The AI industry has convinced everyone that the model is the product. It's not. The model is a commodity. Your data pipeline is the product.

I swapped GPT-4, Claude, and Gemini on the same agent pipeline. Performance barely moved. You know what actually made a difference? Cleaning up the garbage data flowing into the context window.

But nobody wants to hear that. "We switched to the latest model and saw 20% improvement" makes a great LinkedIn post. "We spent a week fixing our data pipeline" does not.

So teams keep chasing model releases like they're going to be the thing that finally makes their agent work. New model drops. Everyone benchmarks it. Marginal improvement. Back to waiting for the next release.

Meanwhile, their retrieval index has duplicate documents. Their CRM data hasn't been validated in months. Their API responses are being shoved into the context window raw, with all the HTML tags and formatting garbage still attached.

The model is doing its best with trash inputs and everyone's blaming the model.

posts.title: Content that drives pipeline looks nothing like thought leadership
posts.hook: The blog post that generated the most inbound pipeline last quarter was 400 words with a screenshot of a Clay table.
posts.body:
The blog post that generated the most inbound pipeline last quarter was 400 words with a screenshot of a Clay table.

No framework. No 2,000-word manifesto. No "5 Steps to Transform Your GTM."

Just a specific workflow showing how I enriched a list of 200 accounts with waterfall data from 4 providers, scored them, and pushed the top 30 to a sequence. With the actual column names visible.

Three people booked calls from that post. One became a client.

The pattern I keep seeing with B2B content that actually drives revenue:

✅ Shows the work, not the theory
✅ Specific enough that someone could replicate it
✅ Short enough that a busy VP reads the whole thing
✅ Has a visual. Screenshot, diagram, Loom. Something concrete.

Meanwhile, the "thought leadership" posts with polished graphics and abstract frameworks get likes from peers and zero pipeline.

Content strategy for GTM isn't a publishing calendar. It's documenting what you build and letting the right people find it. The SEO compounds, the specificity filters for quality leads, and the work itself becomes the proof.

I publish something almost every day now. Most of it is just... what I built that day. Turns out that's more valuable than any content strategy deck.
posts.cta: What's the most unexpectedly effective piece of content you've published?

inspired_by.author: Ann Handley
inspired_by.summary: Ann discusses content strategy tailored for B2B GTM motions, focusing on creating assets that drive pipeline through educational value over promotional hype. She shares a 5-part framework for content ops integration with sales enablement. Examples include LinkedIn-native content that boosts outbound effectiveness.

remixes.label: show-dont-tell
remixes.hook: I stopped writing thought leadership and started screenshotting my actual workflows. Pipeline went up.
remixes.body:
I stopped writing thought leadership and started screenshotting my actual workflows. Pipeline went up.

For six months I was publishing polished GTM frameworks. "The 4 Pillars of Modern Outbound." "How to Build a Scoring Model That Actually Works." Decent engagement. Zero pipeline.

Then I got lazy one Tuesday and posted a screenshot of a Clay table. 200 accounts, waterfall enrichment from 4 providers, scored and pushed to a sequence. I wrote maybe 400 words explaining what each column did.

Three people DMed me that week asking if I could build the same thing for them.

The difference is specificity. Frameworks feel safe because they're abstract. Nobody can poke holes in "align your ICP with your value prop." But nobody acts on it either.

A screenshot with visible column names, real filters, actual data flow? That's proof of work. People can see themselves in it. They can point at a column and say "I need that."

Now my content calendar is 80% workflow walkthroughs and 20% perspective pieces. The walkthroughs drive pipeline. The perspective pieces drive followers. Both matter, but I stopped confusing which does what.
remixes.cta: What ratio of tactical vs. thought leadership content actually drives results for you?

remixes.label: anti-framework
remixes.hook: The content marketing playbook says write long, authoritative, SEO-optimized guides. My CRM says the opposite.
remixes.body:
The content marketing playbook says write long, authoritative, SEO-optimized guides. My CRM says the opposite.

I tracked every piece of content I published over the last two quarters against actual pipeline generated. Not impressions, not engagement. Real conversations that turned into revenue.

The winners had three things in common. They were short. They showed a real tool or workflow. And they included something you could steal immediately, not next quarter after a planning session.

The losers? Every single "comprehensive guide" I wrote. Every framework post. Every piece where I tried to sound like a thought leader instead of showing what I actually built that week.

Here's what I think is happening. Your audience already has enough frameworks. They're drowning in strategic advice. What they don't have is someone showing them the exact columns in a spreadsheet, the actual automation logic, the specific sequence that converted.

When you show the work, you're doing two things. You're proving you can actually do the thing. And you're giving people a reason to reach out, because now they know what to ask for.

I'm not saying stop writing long-form content. I'm saying stop expecting it to drive pipeline. Use it for SEO and credibility. Use show-your-work posts for pipeline.
remixes.cta: Pull up your CRM. What content actually sourced your last 3 deals?

posts.title: I left my job and the first month was mostly debugging cron jobs
posts.hook: The glamorous version of going independent: wake up, build what you want, own your time.
posts.body:
The glamorous version of going independent: wake up, build what you want, own your time.

The actual version: wake up, realize your daily cron failed at 3am, spend two hours figuring out why a launchd plist stopped firing, then debug a Python script that was writing to a JSON file that hit a race condition.

I went back on my own recently after years in revenue roles. The vision was clear. Build a GTM operating system, automate the pipeline work I used to do manually, ship tools that solve real problems.

Week 1 reality: setting up a Mac Mini as an always-on server, writing bash scripts, configuring git hooks, and learning that "production infrastructure" on a solo budget means your laptop is the data center.

Week 2: first automation pipeline working. Blog posts generating themselves. SEO tracking running nightly. Small wins.

Week 3: things breaking in new ways. Token costs adding up. Realizing that building for yourself is harder than building for a company because there's no one to tell you when to stop scoping.

A month in, the system works. Crons fire. Content publishes. Data flows. But the path here looked nothing like the LinkedIn version of "I quit my job to build."

It looked like a terminal window at midnight and a lot of git commits nobody will ever see.
posts.cta: For the builders who went independent... what surprised you most about the first month?

inspired_by.author: Daniel Vassallo
inspired_by.summary: Daniel details leaving a high-paying AWS job to become a solo indie maker, shipping micro-products and sharing revenue updates publicly. He advises on the freedom of small bets over big swings. Post focuses on sustainable building without burnout.

remixes.label: unglamorous-founder-diary
remixes.hook: Day 23 of being independent. I have not once "crushed it." I have, however, restarted a cron job 14 times.
remixes.body:
Day 23 of being independent. I have not once "crushed it." I have, however, restarted a cron job 14 times.

The LinkedIn version of going solo: post a sunrise photo, write about freedom, get congratulatory comments from people who are quietly terrified of doing the same thing.

The real version: your daily automation broke at 3am because a JSON file hit a race condition. Your launchd plist stopped firing and nobody noticed for two days. You spent Tuesday morning debugging Python instead of doing client work.

Here's what nobody told me about going independent after years in revenue roles.

You become your own IT department on day one. Not eventually. Immediately. The infrastructure that keeps your business running is held together by scripts you wrote at midnight, and when they break, there's no Slack channel to escalate to.

You also realize how much of your previous job's value was just being present in meetings where decisions happened. Solo, you have to manufacture those touchpoints from scratch.

But the thing that surprised me most? The debugging is actually the good part. Fixing your own broken automation at 6am feels different than fixing someone else's Salesforce workflow. The ownership changes the math entirely.

I'm not complaining. I chose this. But the highlight reel version of going independent is doing everyone a disservice.
remixes.cta: What's one thing about going solo that LinkedIn doesn't talk about?

remixes.label: infrastructure-confession
remixes.hook: I built a 6-figure consulting pipeline. It runs on cron jobs, a single Mac Mini, and hope.
remixes.body:
I built a 6-figure consulting pipeline. It runs on cron jobs, a single Mac Mini, and hope.

When I left my full-time role, I had this vision of a clean, professional operation. Proper infrastructure. Monitoring dashboards. The whole setup.

Instead, I have a Mac Mini that runs 24/7, a collection of Python scripts triggered by launchd, and a JSON file that occasionally corrupts because two processes write to it at the same time.

The first month wasn't strategy sessions and client calls. It was SSH-ing into my own machine at midnight to figure out why the enrichment pipeline stopped. It was learning that launchd plists have a KeepAlive flag that I should have set from the start. It was discovering that a race condition in a 40-line script could knock out my entire daily workflow.

Here's the thing though. Every hour I spent debugging was an hour building something I own. When I fixed the cron, it stayed fixed. When I solved the race condition, that solution compounds forever.

In my old role, I'd fix a Salesforce flow and some admin would break it next quarter. Now the infrastructure is mine. The fragility is mine too, but so is the upside.

Month one wasn't glamorous. But every piece of plumbing I laid is still running.

posts.title: No-code AI tools have a ceiling and most teams hit it at month 3
posts.hook: Every no-code AI tool demo looks incredible. Then you try to handle an edge case.
posts.body:
Every no-code AI tool demo looks incredible. Then you try to handle an edge case.

Month 1: "This is amazing, we automated 70% of the workflow."
Month 2: "We need a custom integration for this one data source."
Month 3: "We're hitting rate limits, the output format changed, and we need conditional logic the platform doesn't support."

I've watched this cycle play out with teams adopting AI workflow tools. The initial time-to-value is real. You can build something functional in hours instead of days. That's not hype.

But the ceiling hits fast when you need:

... error handling beyond "retry 3 times"
... custom data transformations between steps
... version control on your workflows
... observability into why a run failed at step 7

The teams that navigate this well use no-code to prototype and code to productionize. Build the proof of concept in the visual builder. Validate the workflow works. Then rebuild the critical path in actual code where you control every variable.

The teams that struggle try to stretch the no-code tool past its design limits. Adding workaround after workaround until the "simple" workflow has 40 steps and nobody can debug it.

No-code is a prototyping tool, not a production platform. Knowing when to graduate is the skill.
posts.cta: Where do you draw the line between no-code and code in your stack?

inspired_by.author: Nat Friedman
inspired_by.summary: Nat shares trends in developer tools powered by AI agents, spotlighting new no-code platforms that automate 70% of coding tasks. He predicts a shift from traditional IDEs to agent-driven environments. Includes examples from recent launches accelerating dev productivity.

remixes.label: month-3-wall
remixes.hook: There's a predictable moment when every team using a no-code AI tool starts Googling "Python tutorial."
remixes.body:
There's a predictable moment when every team using a no-code AI tool starts Googling "Python tutorial."

It happens around month 3. I've watched it play out across a dozen teams now, and the pattern is almost identical.

Month 1: euphoria. The demo worked. You automated 70% of a workflow in an afternoon. Everyone's excited. Someone writes a Slack message that says "this changes everything."

Month 2: friction. You need to connect a data source the platform doesn't natively support. You build a workaround. It's ugly but it works. You tell yourself this is fine.

Month 3: the wall. Rate limits hit. The API output format changed and your entire workflow breaks. You need conditional logic that the visual builder can't express. The workaround from month 2 is now load-bearing infrastructure and it's failing.

This isn't a criticism of no-code tools. They're excellent for prototyping and for workflows that stay within their design boundaries. The problem is that nobody talks about where those boundaries are until you've already committed.

The teams that navigate this well do something specific. They use no-code to validate the workflow, then rebuild the critical paths in code once the pattern stabilizes. They treat no-code as a prototyping layer, not a production layer.

The teams that struggle are the ones who chose no-code specifically because they don't have engineering resources. And that's the real trap.
remixes.cta: Have you hit the month 3 wall? What broke first?

remixes.label: hidden-cost-analysis
remixes.hook: No-code AI tools save you from hiring an engineer. Until they don't, and then you need one who also understands the mess you've built.
remixes.body:
No-code AI tools save you from hiring an engineer. Until they don't, and then you need one who also understands the mess you've built.

I've been called in to untangle no-code AI implementations three times in the last six months. Every situation followed the same arc.

Phase 1: the team adopted a visual AI workflow builder. It worked great for the core use case. Everyone celebrated.

Phase 2: edge cases appeared. The platform handled some of them. For the rest, the team built increasingly creative workarounds. Duplicate workflows, manual data patches, CSV exports piped into other tools.

Phase 3: something broke and nobody could debug it. Because the workarounds had created dependencies that weren't visible in any single view. The "simple" no-code solution was now a distributed system that nobody designed.

The cost of unwinding this is always higher than building it right would have been. Not because no-code was the wrong starting point, but because the team never planned for graduation.

Every no-code tool should come with a migration guide. "When you outgrow us, here's how to rebuild in code." The honest ones do. Most don't, because their business model depends on you staying.

My recommendation: prototype in no-code, validate the workflow, then ask yourself if this is a toy or production infrastructure. The answer determines your next step.
remixes.cta: What's your graduation criteria for moving a workflow from no-code to code?

Here's a pattern I see repeatedly with teams adopting no-code AI tools, and a framework for avoiding the trap.

Month 1 is always great. The demo works, you automate 70% of a workflow, the team is energized. Month 2, you need a custom integration. It's manageable. Month 3 is where it breaks down. Rate limits, output format changes, conditional logic the platform can't support.

I recommend treating no-code AI tools as a prototyping layer, not a production layer. Use them to validate the workflow quickly. Confirm the logic works, the data flows correctly, and the output is valuable. Then make a deliberate decision about whether to keep it in no-code or rebuild in code.

The criteria I use with clients: if the workflow has more than two external data sources, requires conditional branching, or runs at volumes where rate limits matter, it needs code. Not because no-code is bad, but because the debugging and maintenance costs at that complexity level exceed the development costs of a proper implementation.

The teams that do this well budget engineering time alongside no-code adoption from the start. The ones that struggle are the ones who chose no-code specifically to avoid engineering involvement. That's where the real mismatch lives.

Every no-code AI tool demo looks incredible. Then you try to handle an edge case.

I'll tell you what actually happens. Month 1: "We automated 70% of the workflow." Month 2: "We need a custom integration." Month 3: "Everything is broken and the person who built the workaround quit."

This isn't a bug. It's the business model. No-code platforms are designed to get you to value fast and keep you locked in. They're optimized for demos, not production. For onboarding, not edge cases.

And here's what nobody in the no-code space will say out loud: the ceiling isn't a limitation. It's a feature. Because once you've built 47 interconnected workflows on their platform, your switching cost is enormous. They don't need to handle your edge cases. They just need to make leaving more painful than staying.

The teams I see struggling aren't dumb. They made a reasonable bet. "We don't have engineers, so no-code is our only option." But that bet has a hidden clause. When it breaks, and it will break, you now need an engineer who also has to reverse-engineer someone else's visual workflow.

That engineer costs more than the one you didn't hire originally. Every time.

posts.title: RevOps without engineering is just reporting
posts.hook: Most RevOps teams are dashboarding, not operating.
posts.body:
Most RevOps teams are dashboarding, not operating.

They build beautiful reports in Looker. They maintain Salesforce hygiene. They run QBR slides. And sales still complains about bad data, marketing still says leads aren't followed up, and CS still finds out about churn from the customer, not the CRM.

The gap isn't reporting. It's plumbing.

RevOps should be building the connective tissue between systems, not just measuring the output. That means:

Automated handoff workflows that don't rely on a rep remembering to update a field. Signal routing that pushes intent data from the website to the CRM in real time, not in a weekly CSV. Win/loss feedback loops that update ICP scoring without a quarterly manual review.

I used to sit in RevOps-adjacent roles watching the same problems recur every quarter. Pipeline data was stale. Lead routing was wrong. Handoffs dropped. And the fix was always "let's add another dashboard."

Dashboards measure symptoms. Engineering fixes causes.

The RevOps teams that actually move revenue have someone who can write a webhook, build an enrichment pipeline, or script a Salesforce automation. Not because they need a full-time engineer. Because the difference between "we see the problem" and "we fixed the problem" is usually 50 lines of code.
posts.cta: Does your RevOps team build, or just report?

inspired_by.author: Dan Disney
inspired_by.summary: Dan breaks down revenue operations as the glue between sales, marketing, and customer success, sharing a playbook for unifying data silos and optimizing the full funnel. He highlights case studies where RevOps transformations led to 30% efficiency gains. Practical advice on tools like Salesforce and Clari for sales ops leaders.

remixes.label: plumbing-metaphor
remixes.hook: Your RevOps team can tell you exactly how many MQLs converted last quarter. They can't tell you why 40% of leads never got a follow-up.
remixes.body:
Your RevOps team can tell you exactly how many MQLs converted last quarter. They can't tell you why 40% of leads never got a follow-up.

That gap is the entire problem.

RevOps has become synonymous with reporting. Build the Looker dashboard. Maintain the Salesforce fields. Run the QBR slides. And all of that is necessary. But it's the equivalent of installing security cameras without locks on the doors.

You can see everything. You can't fix anything.

The teams where RevOps actually drives revenue have something different. They have people who can write the integration between Marketo and Salesforce that ensures lead routing actually works. Who can build the webhook that alerts an AE when a target account hits the pricing page. Who can script the data hygiene pipeline that runs nightly instead of relying on a human to clean records manually.

This is plumbing. It's not glamorous. It doesn't show up in QBR slides. But it's the difference between a revenue team that operates on real-time data and one that finds out about churn from the customer, not the CRM.

The uncomfortable question: if your RevOps team disappeared tomorrow, would anything break besides the dashboards? If the answer is no, you don't have an operations team. You have a reporting team with a better title.
remixes.cta: What would actually break in your revenue motion if your RevOps team took a week off?

remixes.label: revops-engineering-gap
remixes.hook: I've never seen a RevOps job description that lists Python, SQL beyond SELECT statements, or API integration. That's why most RevOps teams can't actually operate.
remixes.body:
I've never seen a RevOps job description that lists Python, SQL beyond SELECT statements, or API integration. That's why most RevOps teams can't actually operate.

We've created a role called "operations" and staffed it with analysts. Then we wonder why the operation doesn't run.

The typical RevOps hire can build a Salesforce report, create a HubSpot workflow, and manage a dashboard in Looker. All valuable skills. But when the CRM and marketing platform disagree on lead status, they file a ticket with IT. When routing logic needs to change, they wait for an admin. When data needs to flow between three systems that don't have native integrations, they export CSVs.

The RevOps teams that actually transform revenue have a different profile. Someone on the team can write code. Not production-grade software, but scripts. Integrations. Automations that go beyond what Zapier can handle.

This isn't about turning RevOps into engineering. It's about closing the gap between "I can see the problem" and "I can fix the problem." Right now, most RevOps teams can do the first but not the second.

The fix isn't complicated. Hire or upskill for technical RevOps. One person who can write SQL, build API integrations, and script automations changes the entire team's capability.
remixes.cta: Does your RevOps team have someone who can write code? If not, how do they solve integration problems?

posts.title: The best GTM engineers were the worst SDRs
posts.hook: Not the worst at selling. The worst at tolerating repetition.
posts.body:
Not the worst at selling. The worst at tolerating repetition.

The SDR who follows the playbook, hits quota, and doesn't question the process... that's a good SDR. They'll get promoted to AE.

The SDR who spends their lunch break writing a Google Sheets formula to auto-sort their lead list, who builds a janky Chrome extension to prefill Salesforce fields, who asks "why am I doing this 200 times when a script could do it"... that's a future GTM engineer.

I was the second type. Hated the manual grind. Not the calling, not the selling. The data entry. The copy-pasting. The lookup work that ate 60% of my day before I could actually talk to a prospect.

So I automated it. Badly at first. Google Sheets macros. Then Python scripts. Then Clay workflows. Then full enrichment pipelines.

Now the whole job is building the systems I wished I had when I was making 200 dials a day from a primary domain with no warmup on SalesLoft.

The evolution from sales ops to GTM engineering isn't a title change. It's the recognition that the people closest to the revenue process are the best ones to engineer it. They don't need to be taught what's broken. They lived it.

If you're an SDR reading this and you've already started automating parts of your workflow... you're not a bad SDR. You're an engineer who hasn't switched titles yet.
posts.cta: Any former SDRs turned builders in here? What was the first thing you automated?

inspired_by.author: Alice Hebert
inspired_by.summary: Alice explores the evolution of sales ops into full GTM engineering, with tips on automating outbound at scale while maintaining compliance and personalization. She references real metrics from hyper-growth SaaS companies achieving 3x pipeline growth. Includes a downloadable template for ops playbooks.

remixes.label: misfit-to-builder
remixes.hook: The worst SDRs I ever managed are now the best GTM engineers I know. That's not a coincidence.
remixes.body:
The worst SDRs I ever managed are now the best GTM engineers I know. That's not a coincidence.

They were terrible at the job in a specific way. They couldn't tolerate doing the same thing 200 times when a script could do it in 10 seconds. They questioned every manual step. They built unauthorized tools during their lunch break.

One of them got written up for spending a week building a Chrome extension to prefill Salesforce fields instead of making calls. Another got a coaching session because they automated their own lead scoring instead of following the manual process.

From a management perspective, these were problem employees. They weren't following the playbook. They weren't hitting activity metrics. They were, in the most literal sense, bad at the job.

But they were bad at the job because they were solving a different problem. They weren't optimizing for calls made. They were optimizing for friction removed.

The SDR role is one of the few in B2B where you experience every broken workflow firsthand. Bad data, clunky tools, manual processes that should have been automated years ago. Most SDRs tolerate it. A few can't, and those are the ones who become dangerous once you give them a code editor.

The career path isn't SDR to AE. For some, it's SDR to engineer. We just don't have the org chart for it yet.
remixes.cta: Were you the SDR who couldn't stop building shortcuts? What do you do now?

remixes.label: pattern-recognition
remixes.hook: If your SDR team has someone who keeps building "unauthorized" automations, don't coach them out. Promote them sideways.
remixes.body:
If your SDR team has someone who keeps building "unauthorized" automations, don't coach them out. Promote them sideways.

I keep meeting GTM engineers who tell the same origin story. They were in an SDR seat. They were doing fine, maybe not crushing quota, but fine. Then they started automating.

First it was a Google Sheets formula to sort their lead list. Then a browser extension to auto-fill CRM fields. Then a full workflow that pulled prospect data, scored it against their ICP, and prioritized their call list automatically.

Their manager's reaction was always some version of: "That's cool, but you need to focus on making calls." And they either did, became a mediocre AE, and eventually left. Or they didn't, got managed out, and went on to build automation tools full-time.

The second path is the interesting one. Because the skills that make someone a frustrating SDR are exactly what makes a great GTM engineer. Intolerance for repetition. Pattern recognition for broken workflows. The instinct to build instead of comply.

Most sales orgs are accidentally filtering out their best future operators. The KPIs are designed to reward consistency and volume. Builders don't optimize for either.

The org chart fix is simple. Create a path from SDR to GTM engineering. Let the builders build. You'll lose a mediocre rep and gain someone who can 10x the output of the remaining team.
